Cost Pricing

Screen Cleaning Cost in BC: What to Expect and When It's Worth It

Published 2026-05-12 · Wagon Windows

Window screens are the most overlooked part of a window cleaning job. Homeowners spray them with a garden hose, they look clean from a distance, and that's where the attention ends. In the Shuswap, that approach leaves a layer of cottonwood pollen and fine organic matter packed into the mesh — and that layer matters more than it looks.

Need professional window cleaning in the Shuswap?

Get an Instant Estimate (403) 837-5092

Screen Cleaning Prices in BC: What You'll Pay

In BC, professional window screen cleaning typically runs $3–6 per screen, with most quality operators in Interior BC — including the Shuswap — landing in the $4–5 range. The price per screen varies with size: standard window screens at the lower end, large patio door screens or oversized specialty screens at the higher end. Wagon Windows charges $4–5 per screen as an add-on to window cleaning service. A medium Salmon Arm home with 20 screens would add $80–$100 to a window cleaning bill — a modest cost relative to the difference it makes in appearance and airflow. Low-end operators often advertise screen cleaning as 'free' or 'included' — and then deliver exactly the level of care that implies: a quick spray that moves dirt around without removing it. Professional screen cleaning involves removing each screen from the window, washing the mesh and aluminum frame with a soft brush and cleaning solution, rinsing, and reinstalling. That process can't be rushed and can't be meaningfully free as part of a bundle.

  • BC market range: $3–6 per screen
  • Wagon Windows: $4–5 per screen
  • Large patio door screens: typically at the upper end of the range
  • Average medium home (20 screens): $80–$100 add-on

What Professional Screen Cleaning Actually Involves

Professional screen cleaning is a three-step process: remove, wash, reinstall. Each step has a right and wrong way. Removal means taking screens out of the window frame without bending the corners or distorting the mesh tension. Screens that have been in place for years can be brittle at the corner splines, and screens from older Shuswap homes often have aluminum frames that have begun to oxidize, making them slightly stiff in their channels. Forcing them causes damage; the right technique is patient leverage at the frame corners. Washing means scrubbing the mesh on both faces with a soft-bristle brush and an appropriate cleaning solution — not high-pressure spray, which distorts the mesh weave and can pop the spline from its channel. The frame channels and corners collect the most debris and need specific attention. After scrubbing, a thorough rinse removes the loosened material completely rather than redistributing it. Reinsertion requires aligning the screen properly in its channel and pressing it flush. Screens reinstalled crookedly allow insects in at the gap and look poor from inside. Taking the extra 30 seconds to seat a screen correctly is the difference between a professional result and a DIY result.

The Shuswap Pollen Problem: Why a Garden Hose Isn't Enough

Cottonwood pollen is the most visible of the Shuswap's allergens, but the pollen problem for window screens is more complex than the white fluff implies. Fine particulate from cottonwood, birch, and alder trees — all common across the Salmon Arm and Shuswap Lake area — loads into window screens through the spring growing season. Under a magnifying glass, a 'clean-looking' screen that's been hosed down once reveals a layer of fine particulate in the mesh weave that the hose rinse didn't reach. That layer does two things: it reduces airflow through the screen (measurably, in well-used windows), and it acts as a substrate for secondary buildup. Pollen, dust, and moisture bond into the existing organic layer, creating a progressively denser blockage that accumulates season by season. For homes with anyone sensitive to pollen or respiratory irritants, this buildup has real air quality implications — particularly on windows that are frequently open in spring and early summer. Removing that layer with a proper brush wash rather than a spray rinse produces a different result. For homes near Shuswap Lake, there's an additional factor: fine mineral particulate in lake air deposits on screens and contributes to a hard-to-clean layer that's part mineral, part organic. These screens benefit from professional cleaning even when they look acceptable from a distance.

Aluminum Frame Corrosion in Lake-Adjacent Shuswap Homes

Aluminum screen frames on properties near Shuswap Lake face a specific challenge: mineral-rich air accelerates the oxidation process that produces the chalky white powder (aluminum oxide) visible on older frames. This isn't just cosmetic — oxidized aluminum is slightly porous, which makes it harder to clean and more prone to retaining organic buildup. For lake-adjacent properties in Blind Bay, Sorrento, Sicamous, and along the Shuswap Lake shoreline, frames that have been in place for 5+ years without cleaning often show visible oxidation at the corners and along the frame channels. Professional cleaning removes surface oxidation and the debris it traps, but frames that are significantly corroded may need replacement to maintain seal integrity. The practical recommendation for lake-proximity homes: clean screens twice yearly (spring and fall) to slow oxidation progression. Annual cleaning is adequate for most Salmon Arm inland homes. Inspect frame corners for cracking or distortion; a frame that's pulling apart at the corners or has visible corrosion pitting should be replaced rather than cleaned — a damaged frame allows insects past the mesh regardless of how clean the screen surface is.

  • Annual cleaning for most inland Shuswap homes
  • Twice-yearly for lake-adjacent properties in Blind Bay, Sorrento, Sicamous
  • Inspect frame corners: cracking or corrosion pitting = replace, not just clean

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I clean my window screens myself instead of paying for professional cleaning?

Yes, for light maintenance. Lay the screen flat, scrub gently with a soft brush, dish soap, and water, then rinse thoroughly. What DIY can't match is the systematic approach to heavy cottonwood buildup or mineral-loaded screens — for those, professional cleaning removes material that a household rinse leaves behind.

Do screen prices vary based on size?

Yes. Standard window screens are at the lower end of the range ($4). Large patio door screens, oversized picture-window screens, and custom sizes run higher ($5–6). When booking, mention if you have patio door screens or oversized screens.

Are screens cleaned at the same time as windows?

They can be — and that's the most efficient way to schedule it. Wagon Windows cleans screens as an add-on during a window cleaning visit. The screens come out, get washed while the windows are cleaned, and are reinstalled before the crew leaves.

My screens look fine. Do they actually need professional cleaning?

If they've been in place through a full Shuswap spring without being properly washed, they almost certainly have a layer of fine pollen and organic matter in the mesh weave that isn't visible from a normal viewing distance. Whether that matters to you depends on airflow preferences and sensitivity to allergens.
